title: The Plancherel formula for L^2(GL_n(F)\GL_n(E)) and applications to the Ichino-Ikeda and formal degree conjectures for unitary groups
speaker: Raphaël Beuzart-Plessis
abstract: Abstract : Let $E/F$ be a quadratic extension of local fields of characteristic zero. In this talk, I will explain two ways to compute the Plancherel decomposition of $L^2(GL_n(F)\backslash GL_n(E))$. In both cases, the result involves the image of base change from unitary groups to $GL_n(E)$ and is in accordance with a general conjecture of Sakellaridis-Venkatesh on the spectral decomposition of spherical varieties. We will also give applications of our formulas to the so-called Ichino-Ikeda and formal degree conjectures for unitary groups.

proof_stages.label: Sakellaridis–Venkatesh setup and period-defined local inner products
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes the symmetric space X_n=GL_n(E)/GL_n(F), recalls the Sakellaridis–Venkatesh prediction that its L^2-spectrum should be governed by tempered unitary-group parameters via stable base change, and uses global GL_n periods to motivate the explicit local beta-period inner products that should occur in the Plancherel formula.

proof_stages.label: Two local Plancherel formulas and the formal-degree consequence
proof_stages.summary: The main local theorems are stated: one gives the conjectural SV Plancherel decomposition, while the other gives the same decomposition with an explicit gamma-factor measure, whose comparison yields the Ichino–Ikeda formal-degree formula for unitary groups.

proof_stages.label: Global Ichino–Ikeda application and spectral-transfer reduction
proof_stages.summary: The speaker formulates the Ichino–Ikeda period conjecture for unitary groups, recalls Jacquet–Rallis relative trace formula comparison and Zhang’s spectral-transfer conjecture, and states that Plancherel for X_n	imes X_{n-1} is equivalent to the needed local transfer identity up to sign.

proof_stages.label: Local relative trace formulas for Theorem 3
proof_stages.summary: To prove the equivalence theorem, local analogues of the Jacquet–Rallis relative trace formulas are written on the unitary side and on the general-linear side, with geometric expansions and spectral expansions justified by trivial stabilizers, strong temperedness, and the Plancherel decomposition of X.

proof_stages.label: Comparison, packet isolation, and recovery of spectral transfer
proof_stages.summary: The two local spectral sides are compared using global-to-local transfer input and local Gan–Gross–Prasad multiplicity one, then Bernstein-center isolation and nonvanishing of Rankin–Selberg averaged coefficients identify the Plancherel measure with the expected one exactly when the relative-character transfer constants have square one.

proof_stages.label: Rankin–Selberg functional equation and Fourier-expansion reduction for Theorem 2
proof_stages.summary: For the explicit gamma-factor Plancherel formula, the proof introduces local zeta integrals and their functional equation, rewrites the desired value Phi(1) by applying a Whittaker/Fourier expansion to a lift F on GL_n(E), and reduces the large GL_n(F)-integral to a smaller mirabolic-type integral by repeated Fourier inversion.

proof_stages.label: Whittaker Plancherel, residues, contour shifts, and positivity
proof_stages.summary: The Whittaker Plancherel formula, center reduction, zeta-integral residue regularization, local functional equation, and contour shifting compute the spectral measure in terms of regularized adjoint gamma factors; remaining signs are removed by positivity of the Plancherel measure, completing Theorem 2.

examples.name: The n=2 Fourier-inversion check
examples.note: The speaker specializes the reduction identity to n=2, where the integral becomes an elementary Fourier-inversion statement over the quadratic extension E/F, illustrating why the general reduction follows by repeated Fourier inversion.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker explicitly runs the Fourier-inversion argument in the n=2 case, decomposing the GL₂(F) integral, identifying an inner integral as a Fourier transform, applying inversion over F, and changing variables

passes.reason: the n=2 case is used as the simplest nontrivial base computation illustrating the general Fourier-inversion identity, not as an obstruction or exception.
